retrieved_docs.title: Pregnancy and Viral Hepatitis -- Etiology -- Hepatitis A
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis A virus (HAV) is a positive-sense, single-stranded RNA virus belonging to the family Picornaviridae with a single serotype worldwide. [3] The virus is mainly transmitted through the fecal-oral route and is most prevalent in developing countries with poor hygiene and sanitation due to food and water contamination. The virus can be transmitted through sexual contact and rarely through blood exposure in injecting drug users during a blood transfusion . The average incubation period for HAV is 30 days. HAV has the highest concentration in the feces, serum, and saliva, respectively. HAV is also transmitted through sexual contact. Chronic sequelae among persons infected with HAV have not been reported. Intrauterine and perinatal transmission of HAV is a rare occurrence. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Hepatitis A
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals who provide services to adults with a high risk for HAV infection (eg, non-residential group homes) and those traveling to areas where HAV is endemic should be offered the HAV vaccine 1 month before anticipated exposure risk. Other individuals who should be offered the HAV vaccine are hemophiliacs whether or not they receive clotting products, those who use illicit drugs, men who have sex with men, patients with chronic liver disease, patients who are awaiting liver transplant or are recipients of a liver transplant, and individuals who work with hepatitis virus-infected primates in laboratories.

retrieved_docs.title: Managing Fresh-Frozen Plasma Transfusion Adverse Effects: Allergic Reactions, TACO, and TRALI -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Posttransfusion risk of HBV infection is estimated to be about 1:357,000 to 1:280,000 per transfusion. HIV and HCV are estimated to be 1:1,467,000 and 1:1,149,000, respectively, in the United States. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prophylaxis (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- It recommends HIV testing of the source patient whenever possible. Voluntary testing of the source patient is required, and laws on consent for testing differ by state. A rapid test is mandatory where available. If the source patient reports a high-risk exposure within the preceding six weeks, some guidelines (though not the CDC) recommend HIV RNA assay. HIV RNA can test the patient for infection during the "window period," which is the time frame between contracting the virus and developing an antibody response. However, there have been no documented cases of transmission during this window period. The source patient may test for hepatitis C (HCV) RNA and hepatitis B (HBV) surface antigen.
retrieved_docs.content: It should also advise patients to avoid donating plasma, blood, tissue, or semen until confirmatory negative testing. They should also avoid unprotected sex and pregnancy. Breastfeeding should be temporarily discontinued. Milk can be pumped and stored until testing confirms a noninfectious state.

retrieved_docs.title: OSHA Bloodborne Pathogen Standards -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) targets the immune system causing acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). HIV is transmitted via direct contact with blood, semen, rectal fluids, vaginal fluids, or breast milk from an individual with a detectable viral load. In the workplace, occupational transmission is influenced by several factors, including volume of blood, type of procedure, type of injury, or percutaneous penetration. Compared to HBV and HCV, the percutaneous risk of HIV transmission is the smallest, estimated to be around 0.3%. [15] [16] While there is no cure for the disease, there are antiviral medications that slow the progression. In cases of exposure, if taken within 72 hrs, administration of post-exposure prophylactic (PEP) medications are highly effective in preventing HIV. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 369
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, making it a member of the therapeutic group “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1027
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections and Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are both subclasses of direct acting antivirals. Within the HCV‑specific antiviral subclass, the drugs ribavirin and telaprevir are members, indicating that they are used as direct‑acting agents against hepatitis C virus infection. Rimantadin, on the other hand, is a member of the Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals subclass, and its pharmacologic action includes the physiologic effect of altering cellular synthetic activity. This organization of antiviral agents highlights how specific drugs such as ribavirin, telaprevir, and rimantadin fit into broader mechanistic classes and clarifies the cellular effect associated with rimantadin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are direct acting antivirals, indicating that these medications specifically target viral proteins essential for hepatitis C virus replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections, Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations, Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals, and Neuraminidase inhibitors, direct acting antivirals are all classified as direct‑acting antivirals. Within this class, the HCV antiviral group includes the drug telaprevir. The HIV combination regimens include the fixed‑dose combinations abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine; both regimens, as well as the individual agent abacavir, are mapped to the nucleoside analogue dideoxyadenosin, reflecting their structural relationship to dideoxyadenosine. Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are represented by rimantadin, which produces a physiologic effect described as alteration of cellular synthetic activity. Neuraminidase inhibitors, direct acting antivirals are represented by zanamivir, a neuraminidase inhibitor that may prevent influenza (الإنفلونزا).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 285
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is classified as a member of the group of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, indicating its role within this therapeutic class. In addition to its use against HCV, ribavirin may also help prevent viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Remdesivir, another antiviral agent, may be employed to treat viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Together, ribavirin and remdesivir exemplify antiviral medications that are utilized both for treating specific viral diseases such as HCV and for broader management of viral infections, underscoring their importance in antiviral therapy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: Telaprevir is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of drugs used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct acting antivirals, a category that also includes cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals. Within the HCV‑specific antiviral class, telaprevir is identified as a member, representing a targeted therapy for hepatitis C virus infection. Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals comprise compounds such as rimantadine, which is recognized as a member of this subclass. Rimantadine exerts a physiologic effect characterized by alteration of cellular synthetic activity, reflecting its impact on host cell metabolism. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific antiviral agents are organized within broader mechanistic classes and how individual drugs can produce distinct cellular effects relevant to their therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 56
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as a type of direct‑acting antivirals, and the drug ribavirin is an example of an antiviral used specifically for HCV that belongs to this class. Cyclic amines that function as direct‑acting antivirals are also considered members of the broader direct‑acting antiviral category, and the medication rimantadine (listed as “rimotadin”) is a member of this cyclic‑amine direct‑acting antiviral subclass. In addition to its antiviral activity, rimantadine produces the physiologic effect of altering cellular synthetic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 66
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections, Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations, and Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are all classified as direct‑acting antivirals, indicating that they exert their therapeutic effect by directly targeting viral components rather than relying on host immune modulation. Within the HIV‑specific antiviral class, the fixed‑dose combinations abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine are listed as members of the broader group Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations, reflecting their use as multi‑drug regimens to enhance efficacy and reduce resistance. The nucleoside analogue abacavir is chemically related to dideoxyadenosin, and this mapping extends to the combination products abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine, underscoring that the abacavir component of these regimens functions as a dideoxyadenosine analogue that interferes with reverse transcription of HIV. Rimantadin, a member of the cyclic amine subclass of direct‑acting antivirals, exemplifies the structural category Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals. Its antiviral activity is associated with the physiologic effect termed Cellular Synthetic Activity Alteration, indicating that rimantadin disrupts cellular processes required for viral replication. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific antiviral agents and combination therapies are organized by mechanism (direct‑acting), chemical class (cyclic amines), and pharmacologic effect (cellular synthetic activity alteration), providing a coherent framework for selecting and understanding antiviral regimens in the treatment of HCV and HIV infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 638
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, and telaprevir is a member of this antiviral group used specifically to treat hepatitis C virus infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections” comprises agents such as ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as antiviral medications specifically to manage hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 1
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) constitute a broad therapeutic class for systemic viral infections and are divided into subcategories that include hepatitis C virus (HCV) antivirals (ribavirin, telaprevir), human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) antivirals (fixed‑dose combinations abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine), cyclic‑amine agents (rimantadine, which alters cellular synthetic activity), integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir), non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) (efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine – contraindicated with the proton‑pump inhibitor pantoprazole), nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) (abacavir, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine), nucleosides and nucleotides that are not RT inhibitors (cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, remdesivir),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 0 -- Community 20
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑infectives for systemic use are organized into two major therapeutic groups: systemic antivirals and systemic antimycotics. Systemic antivirals belong to the subclass of direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) and comprise numerous pharmacologic families. Agents for hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection include ribavirin and telaprevir; agents for human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection include fixed‑dose combinations such as abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine, as well as single‑entity drugs such as rimantadine (a cyclic amine), integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir), neuraminidase inhibitor zanamivir, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – efavirenz, delavirdine, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine – and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, abacavir, dideoxycytidine. Additional antiviral groups include nucleosides and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 0 -- Community 6
retrieved_docs.content: Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) form a heterogeneous pharmacologic class that targets viral proteins and includes agents for hepatitis C virus (ribavirin, telaprevir), human immunodeficiency virus (abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine, abacavir/lamivudine), integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir), neuraminidase inhibitor zanamivir, non‑nucleoside reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) such as doravirine, delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesilat, efavirenz, etravirine, rilpivirine, nevirapine, nucleoside/nucleotide reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) including abacavir, tenofovir disoproxil, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, emtricitabine, cyclic‑amine antiviral rimantadine, and nucleoside/nucleotide agents that are not RT inhibitors (ganciclovir, cidofovir). The ATC system further expands the DAA list. HIV infection, frequently transmitted through shared needle use, can be prevented with pre‑exposure or early‑intervention regimens that employ many of the above
